abstract: Significance The present work examines beliefs associated with racial bias in pain management, a critical health care domain with well-documented racial disparities. Specifically, this work reveals that a substantial number of white laypeople and medical students and residents hold false beliefs about biological differences between blacks and whites and demonstrates that these beliefs predict racial bias in pain perception and treatment recommendation accuracy. It also provides the first evidence that racial bias in pain perception is associated with racial bias in pain treatment recommendations. Taken together, this work provides evidence that false beliefs about biological differences between blacks and whites continue to shape the way we perceive and treat black people—they are associated with racial disparities in pain assessment and treatment recommendations.
title: Racial bias in pain assessment and treatment recommendations, and false beliefs about biological differences between blacks and whites
source_display_name: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
rationale_cluster: The article studies individual-level racial bias, beliefs, and perception among laypeople and medical trainees, linking false biological beliefs to biased pain assessment and treatment recommendations. The core contribution is psychological (cognition, bias, perception) rather than medical intervention or health systems research, so it fits best in Psychology.
rationale_field: The article focuses on racial bias, stereotypes, and their impact on perception and judgment (pain assessment and treatment recommendations), examining false beliefs about biological differences between racial groups. These are core topics in social cognition and prejudice research, fitting squarely within social psychology rather than clinical or health psychology per se.

abstract: If self-regulation conforms to an energy or strength model, then self-control should be impaired by prior exertion.In Study 1, trying to regulate one's emotional response to an upsetting movie was followed by a decrease in physical stamina.In Study 2, suppressing forbidden thoughts led to a subsequent tendency to give up quickly on unsolvable anagrams.In Study 3, suppressing thoughts impaired subsequent efforts to control the expression of amusement and enjoyment.In Study 4, autobiographical accounts of successful versus failed emotional control linked prior regulatory demands and fatigue to self-regulatory failure.A strength model of self-regulation fits the data better than activation, priming, skill, or constant capacity models of self-regulation.The capacity of the human organism to override, interrupt, and otherwise alter its own responses is one of the most dramatic and impressive functions of human selfhood, with broad implications for a wide range of behavior patterns (Carver & Scheier, 1981;Wegner & Pennebaker, 1993).For example, self-regulation has been associated with crime and criminal behavior (Gottfredson & Hirschi, 1990), smoking (Russell, 1971), and dieting (Herman & Polivy, 1975).Men with better self-control are less likely to become divorced (Kelly & Conley, 1987).Children who are better at delaying gratification tend to be calmer, to resist frustration better, to be less irritable and aggressive, to concentrate better, and to get higher grades in school than children who are less able to delay gratification (Funder & Block, 1989;Funder, Block, & Block, 1983).Additionally, children who were better able to control themselves could deal with stress better in adolescence and had higher SAT scores when applying to college (Shoda, Mischel, & Peake, 1990).It is clear that self-control is related to success in many aspects of life.Furthermore, the failure of self-control has immense personal and societal repercussions (Baumeister, Heatherton, & Tice,
title: Self-control as a limited resource: Regulatory depletion patterns.
source_display_name: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
rationale_cluster: The article empirically investigates self-regulation and self-control as psychological capacities using experimental studies and psychological theory, published in a core psychology journal. The focus is on individual-level cognitive and emotional processes, fitting squarely within psychology.
rationale_field: The article develops and tests a general strength model of self-regulation and self-control, using classic social-psychological paradigms (emotion regulation, thought suppression, persistence) and is published in Journal of Personality and Social Psychology. The focus is on self-regulation as a social-cognitive process with broad implications for behavior, fitting squarely within social psychology rather than clinical or personality diagnostics.

abstract: The social category "children" defines a group of individuals who are perceived to be distinct, with essential characteristics including innocence and the need for protection (Haslam, Rothschild, & Ernst, 2000). The present research examined whether Black boys are given the protections of childhood equally to their peers. We tested 3 hypotheses: (a) that Black boys are seen as less "childlike" than their White peers, (b) that the characteristics associated with childhood will be applied less when thinking specifically about Black boys relative to White boys, and (c) that these trends would be exacerbated in contexts where Black males are dehumanized by associating them (implicitly) with apes (Goff, Eberhardt, Williams, & Jackson, 2008). We expected, derivative of these 3 principal hypotheses, that individuals would perceive Black boys as being more responsible for their actions and as being more appropriate targets for police violence. We find support for these hypotheses across 4 studies using laboratory, field, and translational (mixed laboratory/field) methods. We find converging evidence that Black boys are seen as older and less innocent and that they prompt a less essential conception of childhood than do their White same-age peers. Further, our findings demonstrate that the Black/ape association predicted actual racial disparities in police violence toward children. These data represent the first attitude/behavior matching of its kind in a policing context. Taken together, this research suggests that dehumanization is a uniquely dangerous intergroup attitude, that intergroup perception of children is underexplored, and that both topics should be research priorities.
title: The essence of innocence: Consequences of dehumanizing Black children.
source_display_name: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
rationale_cluster: The article investigates intergroup perception, dehumanization, and bias toward Black boys using laboratory and field studies within a social-psychological framework, published in a core psychology journal. The focus is on individual-level attitudes and perceptions rather than social structures or institutions, so it fits Psychology rather than Sociology or Political Science.
rationale_field: The article investigates dehumanization, racial bias, and intergroup perception (Black vs. White boys), including attitudes about innocence, responsibility, and police violence—core topics in social cognition and intergroup relations. The journal (JPSP) is also a flagship social psychology outlet, reinforcing this classification.

abstract: One way to make judgments under uncertainty is to anchor on information that comes to mind and adjust until a plausible estimate is reached. This anchoring-and-adjustment heuristic is assumed to underlie many intuitive judgments, and insufficient adjustment is commonly invoked to explain judgmental biases. However, despite extensive research on anchoring effects, evidence for adjustment-based anchoring biases has only recently been provided, and the causes of insufficient adjustment remain unclear. This research was designed to identify the origins of insufficient adjustment. The results of two sets of experiments indicate that adjustments from self-generated anchor values tend to be insufficient because they terminate once a plausible value is reached (Studies 1a and 1b) unless one is able and willing to search for a more accurate estimate (Studies 2a-2c).
title: The Anchoring-and-Adjustment Heuristic
rationale_cluster: The article investigates cognitive mechanisms of judgment under uncertainty (anchoring-and-adjustment heuristic) using psychological experiments, published in Psychological Science, clearly situating it in cognitive psychology.
rationale_field: The article investigates the anchoring-and-adjustment heuristic in judgment under uncertainty, focusing on cognitive processes underlying heuristic use and bias. This fits squarely within research on judgment and decision making and heuristics, a core area of cognitive psychology.

abstract: Americans’ attitudes about the environment are highly polarized, but it is unclear why this is the case. We conducted five studies to examine this issue. Studies 1a and 1b demonstrated that liberals, but not conservatives, view the environment in moral terms and that this tendency partially explains the relation between political ideology and environmental attitudes. Content analyses of newspaper op-eds (Study 2a) and public-service announcements (Study 2b) found that contemporary environmental discourse is based largely on moral concerns related to harm and care, which are more deeply held by liberals than by conservatives. However, we found that reframing proenvironmental rhetoric in terms of purity, a moral value resonating primarily among conservatives, largely eliminated the difference between liberals’ and conservatives’ environmental attitudes (Study 3). These results establish the importance of moralization as a cause of polarization on environmental attitudes and suggest that reframing environmental discourse in different moral terms can reduce the gap between liberals and conservatives in environmental concern.
title: The Moral Roots of Environmental Attitudes
rationale_cluster: The article investigates how moral values underlie liberals’ and conservatives’ environmental attitudes, using multiple psychological studies and moral framing; it is grounded in psychological theory and methods rather than political institutions or policy analysis. Therefore it belongs to Psychology.
rationale_field: The article examines attitudes, moral framing, and ideological differences in environmental concern, focusing on persuasion and polarization between social groups (liberals vs. conservatives). These are core topics in social psychology, centered on attitudes and social influence rather than cognition per se or other domains.

abstract: This research tested the hypothesis that initial efforts at executive control temporarily undermine subsequent efforts at executive control. Four experiments revealed that controlling the focus of visual attention (Experiment 1), inhibiting predominant writing tendencies (Experiment 2), taking a working memory test (Experiment 3), or exaggerating emotional expressions (Experiment 4) undermined performance on subsequent tests of working memory span, reverse digit span, and response inhibition, respectively. The results supported a limited resource model of executive control and cast doubt on competing accounts based on mood, motivation, or task difficulty. Prior efforts at executive control are a significant contextual determinant of the operation of executive processes.
title: Attention control, memory updating, and emotion regulation temporarily reduce the capacity for executive control.
rationale_cluster: The article investigates executive control, attention, working memory, and emotion regulation using experimental methods and is published in a core psychology journal, making its primary contribution clearly psychological/cognitive. It does not focus on economic, sociological, or medical aspects.
rationale_field: The article experimentally examines executive control, attention, working memory, and inhibition, testing a limited-resource model of executive processes. These are core topics in cognitive psychology, with no primary focus on social, clinical, or other applied domains.

abstract: Previous research suggests that decision makers have a tendency to become locked into courses of action—to throw good money after bad in dealing with losing projects. The present study directly compared the effectiveness of several deescalation strategies designed to make decision makers more responsive to the available evidence. Three deescalation procedures were found to be most effective: (a) making negative outcomes less threatening; (b) setting minimum target levels that, if not achieved, would lead to a change in policy; and (c) evaluating decision makers on the basis of their decision process rather than outcome. The theoretical and practical implications of each of these strategies are discussed. A long stream of empirical research has investigated the commitment of decision makers to losing courses of action. Under the rubrics of sunk cost effects (Arkes & Blumer, 1985; Garland & Newport, in press), entrapment (Brockner & Rubin, 1985), too much invested to quit (Tegar, 1980), and the escalation of commitment (Staw, 1976,1981), numerous studies have shown how individuals can become locked into failing courses of action. To date, this literature has isolated a wide variety of determinants of behavior in escalation situations, ranging from psychological to social, organizational, and project concerns not typically dealt with in the economics of investment (Staw & Ross, 1987). Much of the research in this area has focused on the determinants of escalation, and little attention has yet been given to procedures that might help people avoid the escalation trap. This is unfortunate because, just as debiasing research has helped behavioral decision theory provide policy recommendations (e.g., Fischhoff, 1982), deescalation research could potentially aid managers in avoiding the overcommitment of resources. So far, however, only the rudiments of a deescalation literature are in place. In such a stream of research, one might place Tegar's (1980) and Brockner, Shaw, and Rubin's (1979) studies showing that limit-setting can reduce escalation. Also in this group would be Nathanson et al.'s (1982) experiment showing that information about the problem of entrapment can deter individuals from initially engaging in an escalation situation. McCain's (1986) experiment showing the reduction of investment when losses are repeated and clear-cut, as well as Staw and Ross's (1987) more general discussion of possible techniques for reducing escalation are also relevant. Nonetheless,
title: Deescalation strategies: A comparison of techniques for reducing commitment to losing courses of action.
rationale_cluster: The article is an experimental/empirical study on decision-making biases (escalation of commitment, sunk cost) and tests de-escalation strategies, framed within behavioral decision theory and published in the Journal of Applied Psychology. Its core contribution is psychological, not economic or managerial theory, so it fits squarely in Psychology.
rationale_field: The article examines escalation of commitment, sunk cost effects, and de-escalation strategies in decision making, focusing on how to make decision makers more responsive to evidence. These are classic topics in judgment and decision making and cognitive biases, which fall squarely under cognitive psychology rather than occupational psychology, even though managers are mentioned as an applied context.

abstract: Abstract While there is much evidence for the influence of automatic emotional responses on moral judgment, the roles of reflection and reasoning remain uncertain. In Experiment 1, we induced subjects to be more reflective by completing the Cognitive Reflection Test (CRT) prior to responding to moral dilemmas. This manipulation increased utilitarian responding, as individuals who reflected more on the CRT made more utilitarian judgments. A follow‐up study suggested that trait reflectiveness is also associated with increased utilitarian judgment. In Experiment 2, subjects considered a scenario involving incest between consenting adult siblings, a scenario known for eliciting emotionally driven condemnation that resists reasoned persuasion. Here, we manipulated two factors related to moral reasoning: argument strength and deliberation time. These factors interacted in a manner consistent with moral reasoning: A strong argument defending the incestuous behavior was more persuasive than a weak argument, but only when increased deliberation time encouraged subjects to reflect.
title: Reflection and Reasoning in Moral Judgment
rationale_cluster: The article experimentally studies how reflection, reasoning, and emotional responses shape individual moral judgments, using psychological constructs (Cognitive Reflection Test, trait reflectiveness, deliberation time) and lab experiments. This is squarely within cognitive and moral psychology rather than philosophy or another social science.
rationale_field: The article experimentally manipulates cognitive reflection, deliberation time, and argument strength to study their effects on moral judgment, focusing on reasoning processes rather than social interaction or clinical issues. Its core contribution concerns cognitive mechanisms (reflection vs. automatic emotional responses) underlying moral decision-making, aligning best with cognitive psychology.

abstract: Four studies show that the abstract concept of importance is grounded in bodily experiences of weight. Participants provided judgments of importance while they held either a heavy or a light clipboard. Holding a heavy clipboard increased judgments of monetary value (Study 1) and made participants consider fair decision-making procedures to be more important (Study 2). It also caused more elaborate thinking, as indicated by higher consistency between related judgments (Study 3) and by greater polarization of agreement ratings for strong versus weak arguments (Study 4). In line with an embodied perspective on cognition, these findings suggest that, much as weight makes people invest more physical effort in dealing with concrete objects, it also makes people invest more cognitive effort in dealing with abstract issues.
title: Weight as an Embodiment of Importance
rationale_cluster: The article investigates how bodily sensations (weight) influence abstract judgments and cognitive processing, using experimental methods and embodied cognition theory, which are core to psychological science. The journal Psychological Science is also a flagship psychology journal, reinforcing this classification.
rationale_field: The article investigates how bodily sensations (weight) influence judgments of importance, argument strength, and cognitive effort—core topics in cognition and judgment/decision-making, framed within embodied cognition. Although there is some social content (fair procedures), the primary theoretical focus is on cognitive processes and representations. Therefore it best fits Cognitive Psychology.

abstract: Past research shows that self-control is limited and becomes depleted after initial exertions. This study examined the neural processes underlying self-control failure by testing whether controlled, effortful behavior impairs subsequent attempts at control by depleting the neural system associated with conflict monitoring. Subjects either watched an emotional movie normally or tried to suppress their emotions while watching the movie; they then completed an ostensibly unrelated Stroop task while electroencephalographic activity was recorded. The error-related negativity (ERN)—a waveform associated with activity in the anterior cingulate—was measured to determine whether prior regulatory exertion constrained the conflict-monitoring system. Compared with subjects in the control condition, those who suppressed their emotions performed worse on the Stroop task, and this deficit was mediated by weaker ERN signals. These results offer a neural account for the self-regulatory-strength model and demonstrate the utility of the social neuroscience approach.
title: Running on empty: neural signals for self-control failure
rationale_cluster: The article investigates self-control failure using EEG measures of error-related negativity within a social neuroscience framework, grounded in psychological theories of self-regulation. This is squarely within experimental psychology rather than medicine or economics.
rationale_field: The article investigates self-control depletion using a Stroop task and EEG measures of conflict monitoring (ERN, anterior cingulate), focusing on controlled, effortful behavior and its impact on subsequent cognitive control. The core constructs are self-control, conflict monitoring, and performance on a classic cognitive task, which fits squarely within cognitive psychology rather than social or clinical domains, despite the social neuroscience framing.

abstract: Humility is a foundational virtue that counters selfish inclinations such as entitlement, arrogance, and narcissism (Tangney, 2000). We hypothesize that experiences of awe promote greater humility. Guided by an appraisal-tendency framework of emotion, we propose that when individuals encounter an entity that is vast and challenges their worldview, they feel awe, which leads to self-diminishment and subsequently humility. In support of these claims, awe-prone individuals were rated as more humble by friends (Study 1) and reported greater humility across a 2-week period (Study 2), controlling for other positive emotions. Inducing awe led participants to present a more balanced view of their strengths and weaknesses to others (Study 3) and acknowledge, to a greater degree, the contribution of outside forces in their own personal accomplishments (Study 4), compared with neutral and positive control conditions. Finally, an awe-inducing expansive view elicited greater reported humility than a neutral view (Study 5). We also elucidated the process by which awe leads to humility. Feelings of awe mediated the relationship between appraisals (perceptions of vastness and a challenge to one's world view) and humility (Study 4), and self-diminishment mediated the relationship between awe and humility (Study 5). Taken together, these results reveal that awe offers one path to greater humility. (PsycINFO Database Record
title: Awe and humility.
source_display_name: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
rationale_cluster: The article investigates emotional and personality constructs (awe, humility, self-diminishment) using psychological theory (appraisal-tendency framework) and experimental/longitudinal methods, published in a core psychology journal. This squarely places it in social/personality psychology within the broader Psychology cluster.
rationale_field: The article examines how a specific emotion (awe) influences a social-moral trait (humility), using an appraisal-tendency framework and focusing on interpersonal self-presentation and acknowledgment of others’ contributions. This is squarely within social psychology’s concern with emotions, self, and social behavior, and the journal is a core social psychology outlet.

abstract: Causation, Norm Violation and Culpable ControlHuman brains do spectacular things: They solve complex logical puzzles, compose symphonic masterpieces, conceive technological marvels, and create enduring artworks, for starters.But before they can embark on these prodigies, human brains must achieve something that they share in common with all brains-they must evaluate and differentiate which creatures, objects, and conditions will facilitate their prospects and well-being, and which will do them harm.Evaluation is the most fundamental component of human judgment 1 and one of the most important cognitive capacities for survival.Virtually all meaningful human actions are automatically evaluated. 2These evaluative reactions intrude on the judgments and attributions that people make about their own and others' behavior.So, when people make focal judgments about the components of a human act, such as whether it caused a particular outcome, whether the outcome was foreseen or foreseeable, whether the action was intentional or involuntary, and whether incapacities or situational constraints excuse or mitigate it, they are influenced by their peripheral evaluative reactions to the actor, the actor's behavior, or the outcomes that ensue. 3 As a result, when people are asked to identify, for example, the primary cause of an event, they accord privileged status to actions that arouse positive or negative evaluations.In this way, causal attributions reflect a desire to praise or denigrate those whose actions we applaud or deride.
title: Causation, Norm Violation, and Culpable Control
rationale_cluster: The article analyzes how people make causal and responsibility judgments, focusing on evaluative reactions, norm violations, and culpable control—core topics in moral and social cognition. It is framed as a theoretical/empirical account of human judgment processes rather than a purely normative or metaphysical treatment of causation, so it fits best in Psychology rather than Philosophy despite the journal venue.
rationale_field: The article analyzes how people make causal and responsibility judgments about others’ actions, focusing on evaluative reactions, norm violations, and attributions of culpable control. This is squarely about social judgment and attribution processes—core topics in social cognition and social psychology rather than general cognition or philosophy of mind alone.

abstract: Significance Human prosociality presents an evolutionary puzzle, and reciprocity has emerged as a dominant explanation: cooperating today can bring benefits tomorrow. Reciprocity theories clearly predict that people should only cooperate when the benefits outweigh the costs, and thus that the decision to cooperate should always depend on a cost–benefit analysis. Yet human cooperation can be very uncalculating: good friends grant favors without asking questions, romantic love “blinds” us to the costs of devotion, and ethical principles make universal moral prescriptions. Here, we provide the first evidence, to our knowledge, that reputation effects drive uncalculating cooperation. We demonstrate, using economic game experiments, that people engage in uncalculating cooperation to signal that they can be relied upon to cooperate in the future.
title: Uncalculating cooperation is used to signal trustworthiness
source_display_name: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
rationale_cluster: The paper studies human prosocial behavior and cooperation using economic game experiments, focusing on psychological constructs like trustworthiness signaling and reputation. Although it uses economic games, the core contribution is to understanding human social/psychological mechanisms rather than markets or utility in an economics framework, so it best fits Psychology (social/experimental psychology).
rationale_field: The article examines human cooperation, prosociality, reciprocity, and reputation signaling using economic games, focusing on how people use uncalculating cooperation to signal trustworthiness to others. These are core topics in social cognition, social influence, and interpersonal behavior, fitting squarely within social psychology rather than cognitive or economic psychology.

abstract: Significance Scientific communications about climate change are frequently misinterpreted due to motivated reasoning, which leads some people to misconstrue climate data in ways that conflict with the intended message of climate scientists. Attempts to reduce partisan bias through bipartisan communication networks have found that exposure to diverse political views can exacerbate bias. Here, we find that belief exchange in structured bipartisan networks can significantly improve the ability of both conservatives and liberals to interpret climate data, eliminating belief polarization. We also find that social learning can be reduced, and polarization maintained, when the salience of partisanship is increased, either through exposure to the logos of political parties or through exposure to political identity markers.
title: Social learning and partisan bias in the interpretation of climate trends
source_display_name: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
rationale_cluster: The article studies how individuals interpret climate data under partisan bias and how social learning in bipartisan networks affects belief polarization, using concepts like motivated reasoning and social learning at the individual level. This is primarily a psychological investigation of cognition and bias in a political context, fitting best into Psychology rather than Political Science or Media Studies.
rationale_field: The article focuses on partisan bias, motivated reasoning, belief polarization, and how social learning in bipartisan networks affects interpretation of climate data—core topics in social cognition, attitudes, and intergroup/partisan dynamics. The primary contribution is about social influence and identity-based processing of information, not climate science per se, so it best fits Social Psychology.

abstract: When people are the victims of greed or recipients of generosity, their first impulse is often to pay back that behavior in kind. What happens when people cannot reciprocate, but instead have the chance to be cruel or kind to someone entirely different--to pay it forward? In 5 experiments, participants received greedy, equal, or generous divisions of money or labor from an anonymous person and then divided additional resources with a new anonymous person. While equal treatment was paid forward in kind, greed was paid forward more than generosity. This asymmetry was driven by negative affect, such that a positive affect intervention disrupted the tendency to pay greed forward. Implications for models of generalized reciprocity are discussed.
title: Paying it forward: Generalized reciprocity and the limits of generosity.
rationale_cluster: The article uses controlled experiments to study affect, reciprocity, and prosocial/antisocial behavior at the individual level, grounded in psychological constructs and published in a core psychology journal, so it clearly belongs to psychology rather than economics or sociology.
rationale_field: The article examines how prior experiences of greed or generosity influence subsequent prosocial or antisocial behavior toward others, focusing on generalized reciprocity, affect, and social interaction patterns. These are core topics in social influence and prosocial behavior, fitting squarely within social psychology rather than cognitive or general psychology.

abstract: Significance We identify a consistent reduction in the clarity and vividness of people’s memory of their past unethical actions, which explains why they behave dishonestly repeatedly over time. Across nine studies using diverse sample populations and more than 2,100 participants, we find that, as compared with people who engaged in ethical behavior and those who engaged in positive or negative actions, people who acted unethically are the least likely to remember the details of their actions. That is, people experience unethical amnesia: unethical actions tend to be forgotten and, when remembered, memories of unethical behavior become less clear and vivid over time than memories of other types of behaviors. Our findings advance the science of dishonesty, memory, and decision making.
title: Memories of unethical actions become obfuscated over time
source_display_name: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
rationale_cluster: The paper studies individual cognitive and memory processes (clarity and vividness of memories of unethical actions) and their role in repeated dishonest behavior, using psychological experiments. This is squarely within scientific psychology rather than economics or sociology.
rationale_field: The article examines how memory clarity and vividness for one’s own unethical actions change over time and how this relates to repeated dishonest behavior, focusing on memory and decision-making mechanisms. This is primarily about cognitive processes (memory, decision making) rather than social relations or clinical issues. Therefore it best fits Cognitive Psychology.

abstract: People are more forgiving toward transgressors if they see themselves as capable of committing similar offenses, as demonstrated in 7 studies. Methods included hypothetical scenarios, actual recalled offenses, individual and group processes, and correlational and experimental designs. Three factors mediated the link between personal capability and forgiveness: seeing the other's offense as less severe, greater empathic understanding, and perceiving oneself as similar to the transgressor. In terms of predicting forgiveness, it was important that people's own offenses were similar to the target offense in terms of both severity and type. The personal capability effect was independent of other established predictors of forgiveness and was more pronounced among men than women.
title: Not so innocent: Does seeing one's own capability for wrongdoing predict forgiveness?
source_display_name: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
rationale_cluster: The article investigates individual-level psychological processes (perceived capability for wrongdoing, empathy, perceived similarity) predicting forgiveness, using experimental and correlational designs within a personality/social psychology framework. This clearly situates it in psychology rather than sociology or other social sciences.
rationale_field: The article examines forgiveness, empathy, perceived similarity, and judgments of offense severity in interpersonal transgressions, all core topics in social cognition and interpersonal relations. The journal is also a flagship social psychology outlet, reinforcing that the primary framing is social psychological rather than personality or clinical.

abstract: Significance Lower socioeconomic status (SES) has been linked to increased risk of obesity. This relationship is generally assumed to be a product of low financial resources or greater stress associated with low SES that promotes unhealthy diets and lifestyles. We demonstrate here that the mere subjective experience of being lower in SES relative to others is alone sufficient to causally elicit behaviors that may risk obesity (e.g., preference, selection, and intake of greater calories), independent of actual economic deprivation or stress from being subordinated. Among social species, the physiological/psychological systems regulating hunger may have been adapted to be sensitive to perceived deprivation of critical social, material, and symbolic resources that underlie social class in addition to caloric deprivation.
title: Mere experience of low subjective socioeconomic status stimulates appetite and food intake
source_display_name: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
rationale_cluster: The article experimentally examines how subjective socioeconomic status influences appetite and caloric intake, focusing on psychological mechanisms (perceived deprivation, hunger regulation) rather than economic modeling or social-structural analysis. This aligns best with social/health psychology within the Psychology cluster.
rationale_field: The article examines how perceived low socioeconomic status influences eating behavior and caloric intake, focusing on social comparison, subjective status, and their psychological/behavioral consequences. This is fundamentally about social cognition and the impact of social hierarchy on behavior, fitting squarely within social psychology rather than clinical, cognitive, or health-only domains.

abstract: Many bilinguals routinely switch between their languages, yet mixed evidence exists about the transfer of language switching skills to broader domains that require attentional control such as task switching. Monolingual and bilingual young adults performed a nonverbal task-switching paradigm in which they viewed colored pictures of animals and indicated either the animal or its color in response to a cue. Monolinguals and bilinguals performed similarly when switching between tasks (local switch cost) in a mixed-task block, but bilinguals demonstrated a smaller mixing effect (global switch cost) than monolinguals, indicating better ability to reconfigure stimulus–response associations. These results suggest that regular practice using multiple languages confers a broader executive function advantage shown as improved flexibility in task switching.
title: Flexibility in task switching by monolinguals and bilinguals
rationale_cluster: The article studies executive functions and attentional control (task switching) in monolingual vs bilingual individuals using a nonverbal cognitive paradigm, which is squarely within experimental cognitive psychology rather than linguistics or education.
rationale_field: The article examines executive functions (attentional control, task switching, switch costs) using a nonverbal task-switching paradigm, focusing on cognitive flexibility differences between monolinguals and bilinguals. The core constructs and methods are squarely in perception/attention and executive control, which fits cognitive psychology rather than social, developmental, or educational domains.

abstract: There is scant evidence that incidental cues in the environment significantly alter people’s political judgments and behavior in a durable way. We report that a brief exposure to the American flag led to a shift toward Republican beliefs, attitudes, and voting behavior among both Republican and Democratic participants, despite their overwhelming belief that exposure to the flag would not influence their behavior. In Experiment 1, which was conducted online during the 2008 U.S. presidential election, a single exposure to an American flag resulted in a significant increase in participants’ Republican voting intentions, voting behavior, political beliefs, and implicit and explicit attitudes, with some effects lasting 8 months after the exposure to the prime. In Experiment 2, we replicated the findings more than a year into the current Democratic presidential term. These results constitute the first evidence that nonconscious priming effects from exposure to a national flag can bias the citizenry toward one political party and can have considerable durability.
title: A Single Exposure to the American Flag Shifts Support Toward Republicanism up to 8 Months Later
rationale_cluster: The article uses experimental methods and concepts from social/cognitive psychology (nonconscious priming, attitudes, implicit/explicit measures) to study how a symbolic cue affects political judgments, published in a psychology journal. The core contribution is psychological mechanisms of attitude change, not political institutions or systems, so it fits Psychology rather than Political Science.
rationale_field: The article examines how an incidental symbolic cue (the American flag) nonconsciously influences political attitudes, voting intentions, and behavior, focusing on attitudes, social influence, and implicit/explicit evaluations—core topics in social psychology.

abstract: Significance Political polarization on important issues can have dire consequences for society, and divisions regarding the issue of climate change could be particularly catastrophic. Building on research in social cognition and psychology, we show that temporal comparison processes largely explain the political gap in respondents’ attitudes towards and behaviors regarding climate change. We found that conservatives’ proenvironmental attitudes and behaviors improved consistently and drastically when we presented messages that compared the environment today with that of the past. This research shows how ideological differences can arise from basic psychological processes, demonstrates how such differences can be overcome by framing a message consistent with these basic processes, and provides a way to market the science behind climate change more effectively.
title: Past-focused environmental comparisons promote proenvironmental outcomes for conservatives
source_display_name: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
rationale_cluster: The article investigates how temporal comparison framing affects conservatives’ proenvironmental attitudes and behaviors, explicitly grounding the work in social cognition and psychological processes. The core contribution is to psychological theory about message framing and ideology-linked cognition, not to political institutions or policy design, so it best fits Psychology.
rationale_field: The article examines political ideology, attitudes toward climate change, message framing, and temporal comparison processes, all core topics in social cognition and persuasion. The focus is on how to reduce ideological gaps in environmental attitudes via framing, which squarely fits within social psychological research on attitudes and social influence rather than other subfields.

abstract: Significance People often make decisions with consequences that unfold over time. When facing such intertemporal choices, people use different search strategies. We examine how these search strategies differ and how they relate to patience in intertemporal choice. We demonstrate that search varies substantially across individuals and identify two main search strategies—comparative or integrative search. Importantly, comparative search correlates with greater patience and higher susceptibility to contextual influences on choice. We manipulated search using an unobtrusive technique, revealing a causal relationship between strategy and choice. Comparative searchers make more patient choices and exhibit larger framing effects than integrative searchers. An understanding of how differences in psychological processes change discounting can inform the design of behavioral interventions to improve consumer welfare.
title: Search predicts and changes patience in intertemporal choice
source_display_name: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
rationale_cluster: The article studies individual cognitive search strategies in intertemporal choice, their relation to patience, and experimentally manipulates these psychological processes, all within a behavioural decision-making framework. The focus is on psychological mechanisms (search strategies, framing effects, discounting) rather than economic modeling of markets or utility. Therefore it best fits Psychology.
rationale_field: The article studies intertemporal choice, discounting, and how different information search strategies (comparative vs. integrative) affect patience and susceptibility to framing—core topics in judgment and decision making and cognitive processes underlying choice. The focus is on cognitive mechanisms of decision making rather than social interaction or clinical issues, so it best fits cognitive psychology.

title: Perceiving economic inequality in everyday life decreases tolerance to inequality
rationale_cluster: The article concerns how people perceive and react to economic inequality, using an experimental social-psychology framework and published in a social psychology journal, indicating its core contribution is to psychological understanding of attitudes toward inequality rather than economic modeling or sociological structures.
rationale_field: The topic concerns how people perceive economic inequality and how this affects their tolerance of inequality, which is a question about social perception, attitudes, and social cognition. The journal is explicitly a social psychology journal, reinforcing that the primary framing is social psychological rather than economic or cognitive alone.

abstract: Significance Disclosure is a critical element of social life, especially given Internet media that afford many opportunities (and demands from friends, partners, and even employers) to share personal information—making withholding anomalous, conspicuous, and therefore suspect. Seven experiments explore people’s decisions to withhold or disclose personal information—and the wisdom of such decisions. Declining a request to disclose often makes a worse impression even than divulging unsavory personal information. Moreover, those who withhold fail to intuit this negative consequence: people withhold even when they would make a better impression by “coming clean.” In short, people should be aware not just of the risk of revealing, but the risk of hiding.
title: Hiding personal information reveals the worst
source_display_name: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
rationale_cluster: The paper uses experimental studies to examine individual decision-making and impression formation around disclosure vs. withholding of personal information, focusing on cognitive and social-psychological processes rather than institutions or markets. This fits squarely within social/cognitive psychology.
rationale_field: The article examines social impressions, disclosure vs. withholding of personal information, and how others evaluate these behaviors—core topics in social cognition and impression formation. The focus is on interpersonal judgments rather than individual traits, clinical issues, or cognition in isolation, so it best fits Social Psychology.

abstract: Thinking about God promotes greater acceptance of Artificial intelligence (AI)-based recommendations. Eight preregistered experiments ( n = 2,462) reveal that when God is salient, people are more willing to consider AI-based recommendations than when God is not salient. Studies 1 and 2a to 2d demonstrate across a wide variety of contexts, from choosing entertainment and food to mutual funds and dental procedures, that God salience reduces reliance on human recommenders and heightens willingness to consider AI recommendations. Studies 3 and 4 demonstrate that the reduced reliance on humans is driven by a heightened feeling of smallness when God is salient, followed by a recognition of human fallibility. Study 5 addresses the similarity in mysteriousness between God and AI as an alternative, but unsupported, explanation. Finally, study 6 ( n = 53,563) corroborates the experimental results with data from 21 countries on the usage of robo-advisors in financial decision-making.
title: Thinking about God increases acceptance of artificial intelligence in decision-making
source_display_name: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
rationale_cluster: The paper uses experimental methods and psychological constructs (salience, feelings of smallness, perceptions of human fallibility) to explain how thinking about God affects individuals’ acceptance of AI recommendations. Although it involves religion and technology, the core contribution is to understanding individual cognition and decision-making, which places it in Psychology rather than Religious Studies or Sociology.
rationale_field: The article examines how making God salient changes people’s attitudes toward AI recommendations, focusing on social influence, attitudes, and underlying social-cognitive mechanisms (feelings of smallness, perceptions of human fallibility). This is a question about social cognition and attitude change rather than about cognition per se or methods. Therefore it best fits Social Psychology.

abstract: Judgmental anchoring - the assimilation of a numeric estimate towards a previously considered standard - is an exceptionally ubiquitous effect that influences human judgment in a variety of domains and paradigms. Three studies examined whether anchoring effects even occur, if anchor values are presented subliminally, outside of judges' awareness. Studies 1 and 2 demonstrate such subliminal anchoring effects: judges assimilated target estimates towards the subliminally presented anchor values. Study 3 further demonstrates that subliminal anchors produced a selective increase in the accessibility of anchor-consistent target knowledge. The implications of these findings for the ubiquity of judgmental anchoring, its different underlying mechanisms, and comparative information processing are discussed.
title: Subliminal anchoring: Judgmental consequences and underlying mechanisms
source_display_name: Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes
rationale_cluster: The article investigates cognitive mechanisms of judgmental anchoring, including subliminal processing and knowledge accessibility, using experimental studies on human judgment and decision making. This is core cognitive/social psychology rather than economics (no explicit utility/market framing) or business. Therefore it fits best in Psychology.
rationale_field: The article investigates judgmental anchoring, a classic judgment and decision-making phenomenon, focusing on cognitive mechanisms (subliminal presentation, accessibility of knowledge, information processing). Although the journal is work-related, the core contribution is to basic cognitive processes in judgment rather than to work/organizational constructs. This fits cognitive psychology’s focus on reasoning and decision-making.

abstract: Phenomena that vary over time can often be represented as a complex waveform. Fourier analysis decomposes this complex wave into a set of sinusoidal component waves. In some phenomena, the amplitude of these waves varies in inverse relation to frequency. This pattern has been called 1/f noise and, unlike white noise, it reflects nonrandom variation. Latencies in simple computer tasks typically reveal 1/f noise, but the magnitude of the noise decreases as tasks become more challenging. The current work hypothesizes a correspondence between 1/f noise and effort, leading to the prediction that increasing effort will reduce 1/f noise. In 2 studies, the author examined the relationship between an individual's attempts to avoid bias (measured in Study 1, manipulated in Study 2) and 1/f noise in implicit measures of stereotyping and prejudice. In each study, participants who made an effort to modulate the use of racial information showed less 1/f noise than did participants who made less effort. The potential value of this analytic approach to social psychology is discussed.
title: 1/f noise and effort on implicit measures of bias.
source_display_name: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
rationale_cluster: The article investigates implicit bias, effort, and 1/f noise in response latencies within a social-psychological framework, published in a core psychology journal and using psychological constructs and methods. Its primary contribution is to social and cognitive psychology, not to statistics or another field.
rationale_field: The article examines stereotyping, prejudice, and individuals’ efforts to avoid racial bias using implicit measures, and explicitly situates its contribution as an analytic approach to social psychology. Although it uses temporal response patterns and 1/f noise (a methodological angle), the core theoretical focus is on social cognition and bias regulation. Therefore it best fits Social Psychology rather than Methods or Cognitive Psychology.

title: Causal superseding
rationale_cluster: The article is published in the journal Cognition, which primarily features research in cognitive psychology and related areas of mental processes. With no abstract provided, the most plausible classification is Psychology, specifically cognitive psychology of causal reasoning.
rationale_field: The article is published in *Cognition* and the title "Causal superseding" clearly points to work on causal reasoning/judgment, a core topic in cognitive psychology rather than social or other subfields. No clinical, social, developmental, or occupational focus is implied.

abstract: “Tiger Mother” Amy Chua provoked a culture clash with her claim that controlling parenting in Asian American (AA) contexts produces more successful children than permissive parenting in European American (EA) contexts. At the heart of this controversy is a difference in the normative models of self that guide behavior. Ideas and practices prevalent in AA contexts emphasize that the person is and should be interdependent with one’s close others, especially one’s mother. In contrast, EA contexts emphasize the person as independent, even from one’s mother. We find that AA compared with EA high school students experience more interdependence with their mothers and pressure from them, but that the pressure does not strain their relationship with their mothers. Furthermore, following failure, AAs compared with EAs are more motivated by their mothers, and AAs are particularly motivated by pressure from their mothers when it conveys interdependence.
title: My Mother and Me
rationale_cluster: The article examines cultural models of self, parenting, motivation, and interdependence using constructs and methods from social and cultural psychology, and it is published in a core psychology journal (Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin). The focus is on individual-level psychological processes (motivation, perceived pressure, relationship quality) across cultural groups, which clearly places it in psychology rather than sociology or education.
rationale_field: The article examines cultural models of self, interdependence, and motivation in the context of mother–child relationships, focusing on cultural differences (Asian American vs. European American) and how parental pressure is experienced and affects motivation. These are core topics in social psychology (self-construal, culture, social influence, close relationships), and the journal is a leading social psychology outlet, reinforcing this classification.

abstract: Significance This paper examines how detecting harmful intent creates downstream consequences for assessing damage, magnifying its cost. If intentional harms seem worse, society may spend more money on them than on objectively more damaging unintentional harms or on naturally occurring harms. Why might this occur? Various psychological theories identify the cause as motivation; however, the presence of this motivation has been inferred indirectly. Drawing on animal-model research, we present more direct evidence for blame motivation, and discuss how it may help to explain the magnification of intentional harms. This approach acknowledges the nonrational biases in damage estimates and potential policy priorities.
title: Perceived intent motivates people to magnify observed harms
source_display_name: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
rationale_cluster: The article investigates how perceived intent affects judgments of harm and damage, explicitly grounding its explanation in psychological theories and motivation, focusing on individual cognitive and motivational processes. This is core social/cognitive psychology rather than economics, sociology, or law, despite policy implications.
rationale_field: The article investigates how perceived harmful intent affects judgments of harm and blame, focusing on motivational and cognitive biases in social evaluation. This centers on social cognition, blame, and moral judgment processes, which are core topics in social psychology rather than general cognition alone.

abstract: Abstract This study investigated the role of inhibition in second language (L2) learners’ phonological processing. Participants were Spanish learners of L2 English and American learners of L2 Spanish. We measured inhibition through a retrieval‐induced inhibition task. Accuracy of phonological representations (perception and production) was assessed through a speeded ABX categorization task and a delayed sentence repetition task. We used a measure of L2 vocabulary size to tease out L2 proficiency effects. Higher inhibitory control was related to lower error rate in segmental perception. Inhibition was also related to consonant but not to vowel production accuracy. These results suggest a potential role for inhibition in L2 phonological acquisition, with inhibition enhancing the processing of phonologically relevant acoustic information in the L2 input, which in turn might lead to more accurate L2 phonological representations. Open Practices This article has been awarded an Open Materials badge. All materials are publicly accessible in the IRIS digital repository at http://www.iris‐database.org . Learnmore about the Open Practices badges from the Center for Open Science: https://osf.io/tvyxz/wiki
title: The Role of Inhibitory Control in Second Language Phonological Processing
rationale_cluster: The article examines individual cognitive mechanisms (inhibitory control) underlying second language phonological processing, using psychological constructs and tasks, even though it is applied to language learning. This aligns more with cognitive/experimental psychology than with general linguistics or education research.
rationale_field: The article examines how inhibitory control, a core executive function, supports phonological perception and production in second-language learning, using cognitive tasks and focusing on information processing mechanisms. This is fundamentally about attention/inhibition and phonological processing rather than educational interventions or classroom learning. Therefore it best fits cognitive psychology.

abstract: Significance Across eight studies combining archival and experimental methods, we report evidence for a gender bias in how people speak about professionals. Men and women were, on average across studies, more than twice as likely to describe a male (vs. female) professional by surname in domains, such as science, literature, and politics. We find that this simple difference in reference affects judgments of eminence, with participants judging those professionals described by surname as more eminent and 14% more deserving of a career award. This gender bias may contribute to the gender gap in perceived eminence as well as in actual recognition and may partially explain the persistent state of women’s underrepresentation in high-status fields, including science, technology, engineering, and math.
title: How gender determines the way we speak about professionals
source_display_name: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
rationale_cluster: The paper investigates gender bias in linguistic reference to professionals and its effects on perceived eminence, using archival and experimental studies of individual judgments and biases. This is squarely in social/experimental psychology, focusing on cognitive and social processes rather than broader social structures or institutions.
rationale_field: The article examines gender bias in how people linguistically refer to professionals and how this shapes judgments of eminence and recognition, focusing on social perception, bias, and gender stereotypes. These are core topics in social psychology rather than occupational or general psychology, even though the context involves professional domains.

title: Emotional stress regulation: The role of relative frontal alpha asymmetry in shaping the stress response
rationale_cluster: The topic concerns emotional stress regulation and frontal alpha asymmetry, a classic psychophysiological marker studied within biological/affective psychology, and the journal Biological Psychology is firmly in the psychology domain.
rationale_field: The focus on frontal alpha asymmetry as a neural correlate of emotional stress regulation, published in Biological Psychology, indicates a primary emphasis on brain-based mechanisms underlying psychological processes, fitting best with biological/neuropsychology rather than clinical or general psychology.

abstract: Palatable food induces general approach tendencies when compared to nonfood stimuli. For eating disorders, the modification of an attention bias toward food was proposed as a treatment option. Similar approaches have been efficient for other psychiatric conditions and, recently, successfully incorporated approach motivation. The direct impact of attentional biases on spontaneous natural behavior has hardly been investigated so far, although actions may serve as an intervention target, especially seeing the recent advances in the field of embodied cognition. In this study, we addressed the interplay of motor action execution and cognition when interacting with food objects. In a Virtual Reality (VR) setting, healthy participants repeatedly grasped or warded high-calorie food or hand-affordant ball objects using their own dominant hand. This novel experimental paradigm revealed an attention-like bias in hand-based actions: 3D objects of food were collected faster than ball objects, and this difference correlated positively with both individual body mass index and diet-related attitudes. The behavioral bias for food in hand movements complements several recent experimental and neurophysiological findings. Implications for the use of VR in the treatment of eating-related health problems are discussed.
title: Behavioral Bias for Food Reflected in Hand Movements: A Preliminary Study with Healthy Subjects
rationale_cluster: The article experimentally studies attentional and approach biases toward food via hand movements in VR, focusing on cognition, behavior, and their relation to BMI and attitudes. This is core experimental/clinical psychology (embodied cognition, bias modification, eating-related behavior), not medicine or nutrition science.
rationale_field: The study examines attentional and approach biases toward food, their expression in motor behavior, and implications for embodied cognition, using a basic experimental paradigm in healthy participants. The focus is on cognitive processes (attention, bias, action execution) rather than clinical diagnosis or treatment trials, so it best fits cognitive psychology despite clinical implications for eating disorders.

abstract: Significance People often evaluate others in a negative manner when they do not fit the stereotypes that are generally believed about their group. Here, we not only show that political conservatives are more likely to negatively evaluate people who deviate from stereotypes than are liberals, but also explain why. Previous research has heavily emphasized that people endorse stereotypes because they hold negative attitudes toward members of minority groups and because doing so helps to maintain the current structure of society. In contrast to these perspectives, we demonstrate that conservatives negatively evaluate and economically penalize people who deviate from stereotypes because those stereotypes help them to efficiently categorize people into groups, which provides a greater sense of certainty about the world.
title: Conservatives negatively evaluate counterstereotypical people to maintain a sense of certainty
source_display_name: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
rationale_cluster: The article examines how political conservatives vs. liberals evaluate counterstereotypical individuals, focusing on psychological mechanisms (need for certainty, categorization, evaluation) at the individual level. Despite involving political ideology, the core theory and methods are psychological, not political-institutional, so it fits Psychology.
rationale_field: The article examines how political ideology relates to evaluations of counterstereotypical individuals, focusing on stereotypes, attitudes, and underlying motives (need for certainty). These are classic social-psychological constructs involving social perception, stereotyping, and intergroup evaluation, not clinical, cognitive, or occupational domains. Therefore it best fits Social Psychology.

abstract: Whether cleanliness influences moral judgments has recently become a topic of debate in the psychological literature. After the initial report that activating the notion of physical purity can result in less severe moral judgments (Schnall et al., 2008a), a direct replication (Johnson et al., 2014a) with much larger sample sizes failed to yield similar findings. The current paper examines the possibility that only non-conscious activation of the cleanliness concept, as achieved in participants with low response effort on priming materials, can produce the expected effect. An online replication (Study 1, N = 214) provided evidence that, when participants exerted low (yet still acceptable) levels of response effort to the experimental material, cleanliness priming led to more lenient moral judgments than neutral priming. An online experiment (Study 2, N = 440; replicated in Study 2a, N = 436) manipulating participants' effort on the priming task (low vs. high) supported the hypothesized mechanism. Specifically, respondents in the low response effort group were instructed to complete the priming task as quickly as possible without too much attention, and the cleanliness priming resulted in less extreme moral judgments than the neutral condition as expected. In contrast, respondents in the high response effort group were instructed to perform to the best of their ability on the priming task, with a non-significant difference on moral ratings between cleanliness and neutral conditions. In addition to helping resolve the controversy regarding the cleanliness hypothesis, the current paper calls into attention the role of response effort in the execution and replication of priming studies.
title: Does cleanliness influence moral judgments? Response effort moderates the effect of cleanliness priming on moral judgments
rationale_cluster: The article investigates how a psychological prime (cleanliness) and response effort affect individuals’ moral judgments, using experimental methods within a psychological framework and published in a psychology journal, so it clearly belongs to psychology.
rationale_field: The article studies how a situational prime (cleanliness) influences moral judgments and how response effort moderates this effect, focusing on social-cognitive processes (priming, moral evaluation) rather than basic cognition or clinical issues. This fits squarely within social psychology’s work on moral judgment, social influence, and social cognition.

abstract: This article tested the hypothesis that attachment anxiety positively predicts sensitivity to temperature cues, with the aim of integrating research on associations between temperature and intimacy with perspectives on adult attachment. Study I assessed whether attachment anxiety would positively predict preference for warmth following threat. Study 2 hypothesized that, compared with semantically activating the concept of coldness, semantically activating the concept of warmth would increase anxiously attached people�s relationship satisfaction, as a result of associations between temperature and intimacy. This research was a preliminary examination of the relationship between attachment anxiety and sensitivity to temperature cues. The results suggest that individuals with high levels of attachment anxiety may engage associations between temperature and intimacy as a secondary regulatory strategy and may be especially influenced by such associations when evaluating their current romantic relationships. Thus, this research offers future researchers solid ground from which to explore the links between temperature experiences and attachment-relevant outcomes.
title: Warm thoughts: Attachment anxiety and sensitivity to temperature cues.
rationale_cluster: The article examines adult attachment anxiety, temperature cues, and relationship satisfaction using psychological theories and experiments, and it is published in Psychological Science, clearly situating it in psychology rather than sociology or economics.
rationale_field: The article examines adult attachment anxiety, intimacy, and romantic relationship satisfaction, focusing on how social–emotional attachment processes are influenced by temperature cues. These are core topics in social psychology, particularly social cognition and close relationships, rather than clinical or cognitive psychology.

abstract: The study underscored the importance of contextual factors in understanding age differences in prosocial behaviors such as donation.
title: Are Older Adults More Willing to Donate? The Roles of Donation Form and Social Relationship
source_display_name: The Journals of Gerontology Series B
rationale_cluster: The article examines age differences in prosocial behavior (willingness to donate) and how contextual factors like donation form and social relationship influence this, which is squarely in psychological research on prosociality and aging rather than sociology or economics.
rationale_field: The article examines age differences in prosocial behavior (donation) and how social relationship and contextual factors shape this behavior, which fits core topics in social psychology (prosocial behavior, social context, social relationships). The gerontology context does not change that the main theoretical focus is on social behavior and its determinants.

title: Converging Multi-modal Evidence for Implicit Threat-Related Bias in Pediatric Anxiety Disorders
source_display_name: Research on Child and Adolescent Psychopathology
rationale_cluster: The article concerns implicit threat-related bias in pediatric anxiety disorders, a topic squarely within clinical/developmental psychology and psychopathology, and the journal is a psychology/psychopathology outlet.
rationale_field: The focus is on anxiety disorders in children (pediatric anxiety), which is a form of psychopathology, and the journal is specifically about child and adolescent psychopathology, indicating a clinical/diagnostic orientation rather than basic cognitive or developmental processes alone.

abstract: Previous research demonstrated that if attention is paid to a supraliminally presented number, a subsequent quantitative estimate assimilates towards this number (the anchor effect). One explanation states that this effect is merely caused by the heightened accessibility level of the anchor value itself. Based on this numeric priming account and generalizing from subliminal priming studies, we expected a short-lived subliminal anchor effect. We presented participants subliminally with a low or high anchor value (10 or 90) and next they had to estimate the probability of an epidemic. Half of them were pressed to do this quickly. Only under time pressure, a significant anchor effect emerged.
title: Subliminal anchoring: The effects of subliminally presented numbers on probability estimates
rationale_cluster: The article experimentally studies subliminal anchoring effects on probability estimates, grounded in priming and judgment/decision processes, and is published in a social psychology journal. Its core contribution is to psychological theory and mechanisms of cognition under time pressure, fitting squarely within psychology rather than economics or sociology.
rationale_field: The study examines how subliminally presented numerical anchors influence subsequent probability estimates, focusing on priming, attention, and judgment under time pressure—core topics in judgment and decision making and cognitive processes. Although published in a social psychology journal, the theoretical focus is on cognitive mechanisms (accessibility, priming) rather than social interaction or group processes, so it best fits cognitive psychology.

abstract: Perceptual systems often force systematically biased interpretations upon sensory input. These interpretations are obligatory, inaccessible to conscious control, and prevent observers from perceiving alternative percepts. Here we report a similarly impenetrable phenomenon in the domain of language, where the syntactic system prevents listeners from detecting a simple perceptual pattern. Healthy human adults listened to three-word sequences conforming to patterns readily learned even by honeybees, rats, and sleeping human neonates. Specifically, sequences either started or ended with two words from the same syntactic category (e.g., noun–noun–verb or verb–verb–noun). Although participants readily processed the categories and learned repetition patterns over nonsyntactic categories (e.g., animal–animal–clothes), they failed to learn the repetition pattern over syntactic categories, even when explicitly instructed to look for it. Further experiments revealed that participants successfully learned the repetition patterns only when they were consistent with syntactically possible structures, irrespective of whether these structures were attested in English or in other languages unknown to the participants. When the repetition patterns did not match such syntactically possible structures, participants failed to learn them. Our results suggest that when human adults hear a string of nouns and verbs, their syntactic system obligatorily attempts an interpretation (e.g., in terms of subjects, objects, and predicates). As a result, subjects fail to perceive the simpler pattern of repetitions—a form of syntax-induced pattern deafness that is reminiscent of how other perceptual systems force specific interpretations upon sensory input.
title: Syntax-induced pattern deafness
source_display_name: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
rationale_cluster: The article investigates how adult language users process syntactic categories and patterns, focusing on obligatory syntactic interpretation and its effects on pattern learning. This is core psycholinguistic research on language processing and perception, fitting squarely within cognitive/experimental psychology rather than general linguistics or education.
rationale_field: The article investigates how syntactic processing constrains pattern detection in language sequences, focusing on obligatory interpretations and failure to perceive alternative patterns. This is fundamentally about language processing and the cognitive mechanisms of syntax, fitting squarely within cognitive psychology. There is no social, clinical, or developmental emphasis.

abstract: Burden of Poverty Lacking money or time can lead one to make poorer decisions, possibly because poverty imposes a cognitive load that saps attention and reduces effort. Mani et al. (p. 976 ; see the Perspective by Vohs) gathered evidence from shoppers in a New Jersey mall and from farmers in Tamil Nadu, India. They found that considering a projected financial decision, such as how to pay for a car repair, affects people's performance on unrelated spatial and reasoning tasks. Lower-income individuals performed poorly if the repairs were expensive but did fine if the cost was low, whereas higher-income individuals performed well in both conditions, as if the projected financial burden imposed no cognitive pressure. Similarly, the sugarcane farmers from Tamil Nadu performed these tasks better after harvest than before.
title: Poverty Impedes Cognitive Function
rationale_cluster: The article investigates how poverty-related financial concerns impose cognitive load and impair performance on reasoning tasks, using psychological constructs (cognitive load, attention, decision-making) and experimental methods. Although the topic is poverty, the core contribution is to cognitive psychology rather than to economics or sociology.
rationale_field: The article investigates how financial strain affects performance on spatial and reasoning tasks, focusing on cognitive load, attention, and reasoning rather than social attitudes or clinical outcomes. The core theoretical contribution concerns how poverty-related concerns impair cognitive functioning and decision-making, which fits squarely within cognitive psychology. Although the topic is socio-economic, the mechanisms and measures are cognitive in nature.

abstract: In 7 different studies, the authors observed that a large number of thinking biases are uncorrelated with cognitive ability. These thinking biases include some of the most classic and well-studied biases in the heuristics and biases literature, including the conjunction effect, framing effects, anchoring effects, outcome bias, base-rate neglect, "less is more" effects, affect biases, omission bias, myside bias, sunk-cost effect, and certainty effects that violate the axioms of expected utility theory. In a further experiment, the authors nonetheless showed that cognitive ability does correlate with the tendency to avoid some rational thinking biases, specifically the tendency to display denominator neglect, probability matching rather than maximizing, belief bias, and matching bias on the 4-card selection task. The authors present a framework for predicting when cognitive ability will and will not correlate with a rational thinking tendency.
title: On the relative independence of thinking biases and cognitive ability.
source_display_name: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
rationale_cluster: The article empirically studies individual thinking biases, their relation to cognitive ability, and rational thinking tendencies, grounded in heuristics-and-biases and cognitive psychology within a psychology journal. This clearly situates it in scientific psychology rather than economics or another social science.
rationale_field: The article examines classic heuristics-and-biases phenomena, rational thinking, and their relation to cognitive ability, all core topics in judgment and decision making and reasoning within cognitive psychology, despite being published in a social/personality journal. The focus is on cognitive processes (biases, reasoning tasks, expected utility violations), not on social interaction or personality per se. Therefore it best fits Cognitive Psychology.

abstract: The authors propose that conflict threatens different psychological resources of victims and perpetrators and that these threats contribute to the maintenance of conflict (A. Nadler, 2002; A. Nadler & I. Liviatan, 2004; A. Nadler & N. Shnabel, in press). On the basis of this general proposition, the authors developed a needs-based model of reconciliation that posits that being a victim is associated with a threat to one's status and power, whereas being a perpetrator threatens one's image as moral and socially acceptable. To counter these threats, victims must restore their sense of power, whereas perpetrators must restore their public moral image. A social exchange interaction in which these threats are removed should enhance the parties' willingness to reconcile. The results of 4 studies on interpersonal reconciliation support these hypotheses. Applied and theoretical implications of this model are discussed.
title: A needs-based model of reconciliation: Satisfying the differential emotional needs of victim and perpetrator as a key to promoting reconciliation.
source_display_name: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
rationale_cluster: The article develops and tests a psychological model of reconciliation focusing on emotional needs, status, power, and moral image, and reports four empirical studies in an explicitly psychological journal. The core contribution is to social/personality psychology, not to sociology or political science of conflict.
rationale_field: The article develops and tests a theoretical model of reconciliation in victim–perpetrator relations, focusing on social identity, status, morality, and interpersonal reconciliation processes—core topics in social psychology.
